intro: Children and other dependents unlock several deductions and credits. Let's make sure you claim everything you're entitled to — these are among the most commonly missed items, especially for newcomers and first-time parents.

screens.title: Which of the following describes your children or dependents in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. This determines which deductions and credits are available to you.

options.label: Child under 7 years old at December 31, 2025
Child aged 7 to 16 at December 31, 2025
Child of any age with a physical or mental disability
Adult dependent — parent, grandparent, or other relative I support financially
No children or dependents in 2025

child_under_7.message: Children under 7 attract the highest child care deduction limit — $8,000 per child under ITA s.63(1). We will confirm eligibility in the next questions.

child_7_to_16.message: Children aged 7 to 16 attract a child care deduction limit of $5,000 per child under ITA s.63(1). We will confirm eligibility in the next questions.

child_with_disability.message: A child with a disability qualifies for an $11,000 child care limit under ITA s.63(1) and may also be eligible for the Disability Tax Credit transfer to you as the supporting parent under ITA s.118.3(2). We will cover both.

adult_dependent.message: Supporting an adult dependent may qualify you for the Canada Caregiver Amount under ITA s.118(1)(c.1) or the Infirm Dependent credit under ITA s.118(1)(d). We will confirm the details.

screens.title: Are you a single parent — supporting a child without a cohabiting spouse or common-law partner?
screens.instruction: Single parents may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount for one qualifying child — worth up to $2,421 federally in 2025 under ITA s.118(1)(b).

options.label: Yes — I am the primary supporting parent without a cohabiting partner
No — the other parent or a partner is also in the household

true.message: You may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount under ITA s.118(1)(b) for one child — this gives a federal credit equivalent to the spousal amount. Important: if you paid child support for this child in 2025 you cannot claim this credit for the same child in the same year.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm no child support was paid for the same child claimed as eligible dependant — ITA s.118(1)(b) and s.118(5) prohibit claiming both

false.message: Eligible Dependant Amount does not apply when a cohabiting partner is present.

screens.title: Did you pay for child care in 2025?
screens.instruction: Eligible child care includes daycare, licensed home care, babysitting, nanny, boarding school, and overnight camps. The child must be 16 or under at any time in the year, or have a disability.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Child care expenses are deductible under ITA s.63(1) by the lower-income spouse. Annual limits: $8,000 per child under 7, $5,000 per child aged 7–16, and $11,000 for a child with a disability. The deduction cannot exceed 2/3 of the claimant's earned income for the year.

screens.title: Do you have receipts from your child care provider showing their name, address, and SIN or business number?
screens.instruction: CRA requires provider identification to support the child care deduction under ITA s.63(1). Without it the claim can be denied on audit.

options.label: Yes — I have proper receipts with all required information
Some receipts — missing SIN or business number
No formal receipts

true.message: Receipts confirmed. Child care deduction is well supported.

document_checklist_add.document: Child care receipts — provider name, address, SIN or business number, and amounts paid per child

partial.message: Request the missing SIN or business number from your provider before filing. CRA frequently audits child care claims and will disallow unsupported amounts.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care receipts incomplete — obtain provider SIN or business number before filing

false.message: Without receipts the child care deduction cannot be supported on audit. Contact your provider immediately. We have flagged this as urgent.

preparer_flag.flag: No child care receipts — obtain from provider urgently. Deduction unsupportable without receipts showing provider SIN or business number.

screens.title: Between you and your spouse or partner, who had the lower net income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Child care expenses must be claimed by the lower-income spouse under ITA s.63(1). There are limited exceptions when the higher-income spouse may claim — for example, when the lower-income spouse was a student or in prison.

options.label: I did — I am the lower-income spouse
My spouse or partner had lower income
Our incomes were similar — not sure who was lower

self.message: You claim the child care deduction on your own return.

spouse.message: Your spouse claims the child care deduction on their return. Share the receipts with their preparer as well.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care deduction claimed by spouse — confirm lower-income status and provide receipts to spouse's preparer

similar.message: When incomes are close your preparer will confirm which spouse had lower net income based on final figures and allocate the deduction accordingly.

preparer_flag.flag: Incomes similar — preparer to confirm lower-income spouse and optimize child care deduction allocation

screens.title: Do you have or are you considering a Registered Education Savings Plan (RESP) for your child?
screens.instruction: An RESP grows tax-free and attracts the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 annually — a free $500 per year per child from the federal government.

options.label: Yes — I contributed to an existing RESP in 2025
I opened a new RESP in 2025
I do not have one yet but I am interested
No

existing.message: RESP contributions attract the CESG of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 per year under ITA s.146.1 — maximum $500 grant annually per beneficiary, $7,200 lifetime. Contributions are not tax deductible but investment growth is tax-sheltered. Withdrawals are taxed in the child's hands.

document_checklist_add.document: RESP annual statement from financial institution showing contributions and CESG received

new.message: New RESP noted. Ensure the CESG application is submitted through your financial institution — it does not happen automatically. The child must be a Canadian resident under 18 to receive the grant. Contributions made in the year of opening are eligible for the grant.

preparer_flag.flag: New RESP opened — confirm CESG application submitted and child meets residency requirement

considering.message: An RESP is worth opening even with a small contribution — you only need $2,500 to maximize the $500 annual CESG. Unused grant room from prior years accumulates and can be caught up at up to $1,000 in grants per year. For newcomers: CESG eligibility begins from the date your child becomes a Canadian resident.

preparer_flag.flag: RESP not yet opened — advise on CESG catch-up opportunity and optimal timing for contributions

screens.title: Does your child have a Disability Tax Credit (DTC) certificate approved by CRA?
screens.instruction: The DTC requires a certified Form T2201 on file with CRA. If your child cannot use the full disability amount, the unused portion transfers to you as the supporting parent under ITA s.118.3(2).

options.label: Yes — Form T2201 has been approved by CRA
Application is in progress
No — not yet applied

true.message: DTC certificate confirmed. If your child cannot use the full disability amount against their own income, the unused portion transfers to you under ITA s.118.3(2). Your preparer will calculate the transferable amount. The $11,000 child care limit also applies.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA DTC approval letter for dependent — confirms Form T2201 approval date and period covered

applied.message: DTC application pending. If approved before you file, the credit applies from the certificate start date. File your return on time — if approval comes after filing, amend the return for the applicable years (up to 10 years retroactively).

preparer_flag.flag: DTC application pending for dependent — monitor CRA approval. File return on time and amend if approved after filing. Retroactive up to 10 years.

false.message: If your child has a prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts basic activities of daily living, they may qualify for the DTC. A medical practitioner must certify Form T2201. CRA approval is retroactive up to 10 years — this is worth pursuing if there is a qualifying condition.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC not applied for — assess child's eligibility with medical practitioner. Retroactive claim possible up to 10 years if approved.

screens.title: Are you currently receiving the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) for your child?
screens.instruction: The CCB is a tax-free monthly benefit for families with children under 18. It is not reported as income but must be applied for and is recalculated annually based on your prior year net income.

options.label: Yes — I receive CCB
I had a new child or arrived in Canada in 2025 and have not yet applied
No

true.message: CCB is not taxable income. However, it is recalculated each July based on prior year net income — filing your T1 return on time is essential. Late or non-filing can interrupt CCB payments.

preparer_flag.flag: File return on time — CCB recalculated July based on prior year net income. Late filing interrupts payments.

newly_eligible.message: Apply for the CCB immediately through CRA My Account or by filing Form RC66. Newcomers must also file Form RC66SCH to confirm residency status. CCB payments are not retroactive beyond 11 months — apply now to preserve your entitlement.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not yet applied for — complete RC66 (and RC66SCH for newcomers) immediately. Payments not retroactive beyond 11 months.

false.message: If you have children under 18 and are not receiving CCB, apply immediately — there may be retroactive entitlement up to 11 months. Your preparer can assist.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not being received — confirm eligibility and apply. Retroactive entitlement up to 11 months.

screens.title: Did you provide a home for an elderly parent, grandparent, or infirm adult relative in 2025?

options.label: Yes — they lived in my home
Yes — I supported them financially but they lived elsewhere
No

lived_with_me.message: You may be eligible for the Canada Caregiver Amount under ITA s.118(1)(c.1) for an infirm dependent living with you. The federal credit is up to $7,999 for 2025, reduced by the dependent's net income above $17,670. Medical documentation of infirmity may be required by CRA.

document_checklist_add.document: Dependent's net income information — required to calculate Canada Caregiver Amount phase-out under ITA s.118(1)(c.1)

preparer_flag.flag: Caregiver amount — confirm dependent's net income and infirmity status. Medical documentation may be required by CRA.

financial_support.message: Financial support of an infirm dependent living elsewhere may qualify for the Infirm Dependent credit under ITA s.118(1)(d). The dependent must have a physical or mental infirmity and be dependent on you due to that infirmity. Your preparer will confirm eligibility.

preparer_flag.flag: Infirm dependent living elsewhere — confirm infirmity status and degree of financial dependence for ITA s.118(1)(d) credit

branch_completion_summary.message:
Children and dependents section complete. Here is what we have identified.

Note for preparer: if the client transferred property or capital to a minor child in 2025, income attribution rules apply under ITA s.74.1(2) — preparer to confirm. Capital gains earned in the child's hands are not attributed; only income such as interest and dividends is affected.
highlighted_documents: Child care receipts — provider name, SIN or business number, amounts paid per child
CRA DTC approval letter if disability credit applies to a dependent
Dependent's net income information if caregiver credit is claimed
Form RC66 and RC66SCH if CCB not yet applied for
